frameworks.name: computational psychiatry
frameworks.one_line: model-based decision parameters as clinical markers of reuse risk

frameworks.name: decision neuroscience under risk and ambiguity
frameworks.one_line: separates tolerance of known risk vs partially unknown (ambiguous) risk

frameworks.name: time-lagged mixed-effects logistic regression
frameworks.one_line: links session-by-session model parameters to prospective opioid use

core_question: Do model-based decision parameters from a risky choice task prospectively predict opioid reuse in patients with opioid use disorder?
key_assumptions: risky decision-making decomposes into separable risk and ambiguity attitudes
session-level computational parameters track current cognitive state
between-session use events can be modeled with time-lagged regression
manipulations: longitudinal repeated assessment up to 7 months in a community treatment setting
risky decision-making task with known-risk and ambiguous options
comparison of patients with opioid use disorder vs control participants
key_findings: ambiguity tolerance, not known-risk tolerance, was elevated prior to opioid use events
ambiguity tolerance predicted prospective use independent of standard clinical factors
patients overall showed higher tolerance of known risks than controls
interpretations: ambiguity tolerance indexes a transient cognitive vulnerability window for reuse
computational decision markers can yield mechanistic, individual-level relapse signals beyond clinical scales
